extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin is a thermal incubator consisting of a styrofoam insulated box equipped with a resistive heat‑bed, an internal fan, two internal temperature sensors, one external temperature sensor, and a Raspberry Pi that runs the controller. The plant is designed to reach and maintain a target temperature regardless of the contents placed in the box. The system operates in a laboratory/biological‑incubation domain and provides a simple CPS suitable for digital‑twin demonstrations.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin offers (i) real‑time monitoring and visualization of temperature and controller state via a time‑series database and dashboard, (ii) on‑demand model calibration using the described calibration algorithm, (iii) prediction of internal temperature through two simulation models (Model A and Model B) and a Kalman‑filter based heat‑bed state estimator, (iv) anomaly detection based on the Kalman filter residuals, (v) what‑if simulations enabled by co‑simulation that can optimise controller parameters, and (vi) a closed‑loop self‑adaptation process that automatically detects plant‑behavior changes, schedules experiments, recalibrates the model, updates the estimator and re‑configures the controller.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation uses a Raspberry Pi for the low‑level driver and controller, RabbitMQ as the message‑broker for synchronisation between components, a low‑level driver abstracting sensor/actuator communication, a bang‑bang style controller with configurable lower/upper temperature limits, heating and waiting durations, a time‑series database (unspecified) feeding a web‑based dashboard for visualization, calibration and optimisation algorithms (including a Kalman filter), and open‑source code and datasets hosted in a GitHub repository (https://github.com/INTO‑CPS‑Association/example‑incubator).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The DT operates in near‑real‑time: it continuously listens to controller and low‑level driver messages published on the RabbitMQ broker, stores time‑stamped sensor data in a time‑series database and updates visual dashboards as data arrive. Calibration, Kalman‑filter based anomaly detection and what‑if simulations are triggered on‑demand or when new data are received, using either live or historical data. No explicit numerical latency, sampling frequency, or granularity is specified in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system follows a publish‑subscribe architecture centred on a RabbitMQ message broker. Physical‑twin hardware (incubator), a low‑level driver that abstracts sensor/actuator I/O, and a bang‑bang controller exchange telemetry and commands via RabbitMQ. DT services such as monitoring, calibration, Kalman‑filter anomaly detection, and co‑simulation subscribe to the same broker to receive data and issue control actions. This centralised broker orchestrates component interaction, enabling modular addition of services and loosely‑coupled integration, as described in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is achieved through RabbitMQ, an AMQP‑compatible message broker. All telemetry (temperature sensor readings) and control commands are exchanged as AMQP messages, allowing any AMQP‑capable client or external enterprise application to publish or subscribe to the same topics. This enables interoperability and integration of additional Digital Twins or enterprise systems via the standard message‑based interface. No other protocols or standards are mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The incubator Digital Twin incorporates two analytical behavioural models of the temperature dynamics: Model A (with two free parameters) and Model B (with four free parameters) derived from the heat equation Q = c·m·ΔT and calibrated against measured temperatures. Model B relaxes the assumption that all electrical power is transferred to heat the air, thus providing higher fidelity for the heat‑bed inertia. Sensor data are collected by three temperature sensors (two inside the insulated styrofoam box, one outside) via a low‑level driver that periodically reads the measurements and publishes them to a RabbitMQ communication server. The controller also publishes its actuation commands (fan and heat‑bed on/off) to RabbitMQ. The DT listens to these message streams, stores the time‑stamped data in a time‑series database, and uses it for on‑demand calibration, monitors, and a Kalman‑filter based anomaly detector. Calibration algorithms update the free parameters of Models A and B in real time, and the calibrated models feed co‑simulation ‘what‑if’ runs and optimisation routines.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation in the incubator is performed by a heat‑bed (thermal heater) and a cooling fan, both driven by commands issued by a bang‑bang style controller running on a Raspberry Pi. The controller publishes actuation messages (turn heat‑bed on/off, turn fan on/off) to the RabbitMQ message broker, where the low‑level driver receives them and applies the signals to the hardware. Control parameters exposed by the controller are a lower temperature limit (LL), an upper limit (UL), a heating duration (H) and a waiting duration (C). The low‑level driver enforces a safety protocol that checks incoming commands before applying them, ensuring the physical twin remains in a safe state.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Temperature sensing is provided by three thermistors: two positioned inside the insulated styrofoam box to capture internal air temperature and one positioned outside to monitor ambient temperature. The low‑level driver periodically reads each sensor, uploads the readings to the RabbitMQ communication server, and timestamps them for storage in the time‑series database. No explicit sampling frequency or accuracy figures are given, but the architecture supports real‑time streaming of the sensor values for dashboard visualisation and DT analytics.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is addressed by comparing Model A and Model B against experimental calibration results (see Figure 13); Model B shows superior accuracy in reproducing the heat‑bed's thermal inertia. Validation is performed via on‑demand calibration that fits the models' free parameters to collected temperature data. Sources of uncertainty stem from modelling assumptions: uniform temperature distribution (enabled by the fan), negligible wall heat storage, constant specific heat capacity, and the proportion of electrical power converted to heat. These uncertainties are mitigated by periodic recalibration and by running a Kalman filter that estimates the unmeasured heat‑bed temperature and detects anomalies. Monitoring components raise violations, prompting re‑calibration or controller re‑configuration, thus constituting a continuous quality‑assurance loop.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Temperature sensors (two inside the incubator and one outside) are read periodically by the low‑level driver. The driver uploads each temperature reading as a message to the RabbitMQ communication server. The messages contain numeric temperature values (in °C) and are sent over the RabbitMQ message‑broker infrastructure. Before publishing, the driver applies a built‑in safe protocol that checks the device state, providing basic data‑quality assurance. DT components listen to these RabbitMQ messages and integrate the data into a time‑series database for further processing and visualisation.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands are sent from the DT (or other software components) to the Raspberry Pi controller via RabbitMQ messages. Command types include heat‑bed activation, fan control, and parameter settings such as lower/upper temperature limits (LL/UL), heating duration (H) and waiting duration (C). The controller validates incoming commands, executes them on the physical hardware, and publishes its current state back to RabbitMQ. The low‑level driver incorporates a safe protocol that ensures commands are applied only when the device is in a safe state, and the controller provides execution confirmation through status messages.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All communication between physical and cyber components is mediated by a RabbitMQ server acting as a central message broker. The low‑level driver, controller, and DT modules act as RabbitMQ clients, sending and receiving messages through the broker. The deployment uses a client‑server topology; RabbitMQ is described as a lightweight, mature, and easy‑to‑deploy open‑source message broker that can run on‑premise or in the cloud. No explicit bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, or security protocol details are provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The controller software runs on a Raspberry Pi embedded computer. The RabbitMQ broker can be deployed either on‑premise or in a cloud environment. DT functionalities such as real‑time visualisation, on‑demand calibration, anomaly detection, and what‑if simulation are implemented as separate software components that listen to the RabbitMQ broker; all source code and datasets are publicly available in an open‑source GitHub repository. The documents do not specify quantitative computational, storage, scalability, or availability requirements.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The DT engineering process for the incubator case study includes: (i) real‑time acquisition of temperature sensor data and actuator states by a low‑level driver that publishes messages to a RabbitMQ message broker, (ii) a controller (bang‑bang style) that consumes these messages and issues heating or fan commands, (iii) on‑demand calibration of the temperature models (Model A and Model B) executed automatically as described in the calibration procedure, (iv) validation of model predictions against measured data during experimental runs, (v) continuous monitoring and anomaly detection using a Kalman filter that also estimates the unseen heat‑bed temperature, and (vi) an iterative self‑adaptation loop that, upon detection of a behavioural change, schedules new experiments, re‑calibrates Model B, re‑configures the Kalman filter, runs co‑simulations for controller optimisation and finally re‑configures the controller. These steps constitute the development, integration, operation and evolution stages of the Digital Twin as presented in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin generates decision‑support insights through: (a) anomaly detection on the temperature dynamics performed by a Kalman filter, which also provides an estimate of the unmeasured heat‑bed temperature, (b) what‑if simulation capability implemented via co‑simulation that consumes historical or live PT data to evaluate alternative controller settings, (c) automatic optimisation of controller parameters based on the outcomes of the what‑if simulations, and (d) a self‑adaptation loop that reacts to detected changes (e.g., insertion of a cold object) by re‑calibrating models, updating the Kalman filter, re‑running simulations and re‑configuring the controller, thereby closing the loop between insight generation and actionable control decisions.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The low‑level driver incorporates a safe protocol that continuously reads temperature data, checks for commands, and ensures the incubator remains in a safe state before forwarding actuator instructions, acting as a fail‑safe safety mechanism. Communication between the controller, driver and DT components is performed via a RabbitMQ message broker, which provides reliable message delivery; however, the documents do not specify authentication, encryption, or other cybersecurity measures.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. **2.2.1** **The Communication Server**


For the case study, the RabbitMQ [4] server was deployed for synchronisation and communication. With tens of thousands of users, RabbitMQ is one of the most popular
open source message brokers. RabbitMQ is a mature, lightweight, and easy to deploy
on premises and in the cloud.

The controller on the Raspberry Pi is listening from the RabbitMQ server for commands of controlling the fan and the heatbed and at the same time sharing the control
states to the RabbitMQ server.
We choose RabbitMQ because it greatly facilitates the digital twinning process, as
the DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages being passed
between the controller and the load level driver, as illustrated in fig. 7.


**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the
